1: The superscription places Psalm 63 "when he was in the wilderness of Judah" — likely the Absalom rebellion when David fled Jerusalem into the wilderness (2 Sam 15-16). אֱלֹהִים אֵלִי אַתָּה (ʾĕlōhîm ʾêlî ʾattāh, "O God, you are my God") — the possessive: not merely "God" but "my God" — the covenant-claiming of the divine name. אֲשַׁחֲרֶךָּ (ʾăshaḥărĕkā, "earnestly I seek you") — shāḥar: to seek at dawn, to search earnestly (the root of shaḥar, the dawn — the early-rising seeker who hunts for God as the dawn hunter hunts for prey). צָמְאָה לְךָ נַפְשִׁי (ṣāmĕʾāh lĕkā napshî, "my soul thirsts for you") — ṣāmēʾ: to thirst (as in Ps 42:2). כָּמַהּ לְךָ בְשָׂרִי (kāmah lĕkā bĕśārî, "my flesh longs for you") — kāmah: a hapax legomenon (appears only here) — to yearn intensely, to long with physical desire. Both nāpesh (the inner self) and bāśār (flesh/body) are thirsting and yearning — the whole person in physical extremity reaching for God.
2: כֵּן בַּקֹּדֶשׁ חֲזִיתִיךָ (kên baqqōdesh ḥăzîtîkā, "So I have gazed upon you in the sanctuary") — ḥāzāh: to gaze, to behold (the prophetic vision-verb — the seer's seeing). The paradox: in the wilderness without water (v1), the Psalmist remembers the sanctuary vision — the past temple experience sustains the present wilderness. לִרְאוֹת עֻזְּךָ וּכְבוֹדֶךָ (lirʾôt ʿuzzĕkā ûkĕbôdekā, "beholding your power and your glory") — ʿōz and kābôd: the two paired divine attributes seen in the sanctuary — power and weightiness/glory. The sanctuary is where God's power and glory are manifested visibly; the wilderness is where the memory of that vision sustains.
3: כִּי-טוֹב חַסְדְּךָ מֵחַיִּים (kî-ṭôb ḥasdĕkā mêḥayyîm, "Because your steadfast love is better than life itself") — the theological claim at the heart of the psalm and one of the most radical statements in the Psalter: ḥesed (covenant-loyalty) is better than (ṭôb min) life (ḥayyîm). Life (ḥayyîm) is the highest human good — the thing that sustains everything else. But divine ḥesed exceeds even life in value. Paul makes the same comparison in Phil 1:21 ("to live is Christ, to die is gain") and Phil 1:23 ("to depart and be with Christ is far better"). שְׂפָתַי יְשַׁבְּחוּנְךָ (śĕpātay yĕshabbĕḥûnĕkā, "my lips will praise you") — the response to such ḥesed: the lips that would be silent in death are impelled to praise.
4: כֵּן אֲבָרֶכְךָ בְחַיָּי (kên ʾăbārekĕkā bĕḥayyāy, "So I will bless you as long as I live") — the pledge of lifelong praise (bĕḥayyāy: in my life — for all its duration). בְּשִׁמְךָ אֶשָּׂא כַפָּי (bĕshimkā ʾeśśāʾ kappāy, "in your name I will lift up my hands") — nāśāʾ kappayim: to lift the palms upward (the posture of prayer and supplication — openness to receive, surrender, appeal). The name (shêm) invoked in the gesture: the hands raised to the Name.
5: כְּמוֹ חֵלֶב וָדֶשֶׁן תִּשְׂבַּע נַפְשִׁי (kĕmô ḥêleb wādeshen tiśbaʿ napshî, "My soul is satisfied as with the richest of food") — ḥêleb: fat (the richest portion of the sacrificial animal — the fat offered to God in Lev 3:16; 7:23; the fat as the best, richest part); deshen: fatness, marrow, abundance. Together they describe the richest possible feast. The nāpesh satisfied as if gorged on the feast of God's presence. וְשִׂפְתֵי רְנָנוֹת יְהַלֶּל-פִּי (wĕśiptê rĕnānôt yĕhallel-pî, "my mouth praises you with joyful lips") — rĕnānāh: the ringing joyful cry (as in Ps 61:1 — the urgent ringing cry). The desert-starved lips that longed for God (v1) are now the lips of feasting satisfaction.
6: אִם-זְכַרְתִּיךָ עַל-יְצוּעָי (ʾim-zĕkartîkā ʿal-yĕṣûʿāy, "When I remember you on my bed") — zākar: to remember (the covenant remembering of God — keeping the divine character and deeds present in consciousness). Yĕṣûaʿ: a bed, a couch (the reclining place — the night rest). בְּאַשְׁמֻרוֹת אֶהְגֶּה-בָּךְ (bĕʾashmurôt ʾehgeh-bāk, "I meditate on you through the watches of the night") — ʾashmurāh: a watch (the night was divided into three watches: evening, midnight, and the morning watch — Exod 14:24; Judg 7:19). Hāgāh: to murmur, to meditate (as in Ps 1:2 — the Torah-meditator murmuring day and night; here the Psalmist meditates on God himself through all the night watches).
7: כִּי-הָיִיתָ עֶזְרָתָה לִּי (kî-hāyîtā ʿezrātāh llî, "For you have been my help") — ʿezrāh: help (the ʿezer of Gen 2:18 — the essential help that prevents incompleteness). The past help grounds the present trust. וּבְצֵל כְּנָפֶיךָ אֲרַנֵּן (ûbĕṣêl kĕnāpêkā ʾărannên, "and in the shadow of your wings I will rejoice") — the "shadow of your wings" (as in Ps 57:1; 61:4): the wings of the cherubim over the ark, the brooding divine bird. Rānan: to shout for joy (in the midst of the wilderness, the Psalmist shouts with joy because of the remembered and promised divine protection).
8: דָּבְקָה נַפְשִׁי אַחֲרֶיךָ (dābĕqāh napshî ʾaḥărêkā, "My soul clings close after you") — dābaq: to cling, to stick fast, to cleave (the marriage-covenant verb of Gen 2:24: "a man shall leave his father and mother and dābaq to his wife"; and Ruth 1:14: Ruth "clung" to Naomi after Orpah departed). The covenant-cleaving that is the deepest expression of love and commitment — the Psalmist's nāpesh clinging to God with the totality of the marriage covenant. בִּי הֶחֱזִיקָה יְמִינֶךָ (bî heḥĕzîqāh yĕmînekā, "your right hand holds me fast") — ḥāzaq (hiphil: to hold firmly, to grip): God's right hand (yāmîn) maintaining the grip on the one who clings. The covenant is mutual: the Psalmist clings to God, and God holds the Psalmist. Neither releases the other.
9: וְהֵמָּה לְשׁוֹאָה יְבַקְשׁוּ נַפְשִׁי (wĕhêmmāh lĕshôʾāh yĕbaqshû napshî, "But those who seek to destroy my life") — shôʾāh: devastation, destruction (a word for total ruin — storm and desolation); bāqash: to seek (as in Ps 27:4 — but here in the hostile sense: to hunt). יָבֹאוּ בְּתַחְתִּיּוֹת הָאָרֶץ (yābōʾû bĕtaḥtiyyôt hāʾāreṣ, "shall descend into the depths of the earth") — taḥtiyyôt hāʾāreṣ: the lower parts of the earth (the underworld, Sheol — as in Ps 86:13: "you have delivered my soul from the depth of Sheol"; Ezek 26:20). The reversal: those who seek the Psalmist's destruction descend to the place they sought to send him.
10: יַגִּירֻהוּ עַל-יְדֵי-חָרֶב (yaggîruhû ʿal-yĕdê-ḥāreb, "They will be delivered over to the sword") — gārar (hiphil: to drag, to hand over — the enemies delivered into the power of the sword). מְנָת שֻׁעָלִים יִהְיוּ (mĕnāt shûʿālîm yihyû, "they will become prey for jackals") — mĕnāh: a portion, an allotted share; shûʿālîm: jackals (the scavengers of the battlefield and the wilderness). The enemies become the jackals' food portion — the carrion-eaters taking their allotted share of the fallen. The irony: those who sought to destroy the Psalmist's nāpesh (v9) become the animals' food portion (mĕnāh).
11: וְהַמֶּלֶךְ יִשְׂמַח בֵּאלֹהִים (wĕhammelek yiśmaḥ bêʾlōhîm, "But the king will rejoice in God") — the psalm's closing affirmation: the king (David, the Psalmist-king) rejoices in God. כָּל-הַנִּשְׁבָּע בּוֹ יִתְהַלֵּל (kol-hannishbāʿ bô yithalêl, "all who swear by him will boast in him") — nishbāʿ bô: "swear by him" — to take an oath in the name of the LORD is to invoke him as the witness and guarantor of truth (Gen 21:23; 1 Sam 20:42). Those who swear by the LORD identify themselves with him and find their boast in him alone. כִּי יִסָּכֵר פִּי דוֹבְרֵי-שָׁקֶר (kî yissākêr pî dôbĕrê-shāqer, "for the mouths of liars will be silenced") — sākar (niphal: to be stopped, to be plugged — as a stream is blocked): the lying mouths silenced as justice restores truthful speech to the public square.
